title: The Bishop Orders His Tomb At Saint Praxedן¿½s Church Rome
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vanity, saith the preacher, vanity!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Draw round my bed: is Anselm keeping back?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nephews, sons mine . . . ah God, I know not! Well,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She, men would have to be your mother once,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Gandolf envied me, so fair she was!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whatן¿½s done is done, and she is dead beside,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dead long ago, and I am Bishop since,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as she died so must we die ourselves,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thence ye may perceive the worldן¿½s a dream.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Life, how and what is it? As here I lie
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In this state-chamber, dying by degrees,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hours and long hours in the dead night, I ask
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Do I live, am I dead?ן¿½ Peace, peace seems all.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saint Praxedן¿½s ever was the church for peace;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so, about this tomb of mine. I fought
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With tooth and nail to save my niche, ye know:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Gandolf cozened me, despite my care;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shrewd was that snatch from out the corner South
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He graced his carrion with, God curse the same!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet still my niche is not so cramped but thence
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One sees the pulpit oן¿½ the epistle-side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And somewhat of the choir, those silent seats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And up into the aery dome where live
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The angels, and a sunbeamן¿½s sure to lurk:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I shall fill my slab of basalt there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½neath my tabernacle take my rest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With those nine columns round me, two and two,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The odd one at my feet where Anselm stands:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Peach-blossom marble all, the rare, the ripe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As fresh-poured red wine of a mighty pulse.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Gandolf with his paltry onion-stone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Put me where I may look at him! True peach,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rosy and flawless: how I earned the prize!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Draw close: that conflagration of my church
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What then? So much was saved if aught were missed!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My sons, ye would not be my death? Go dig
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The white-grape vineyard where the oil-press stood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Drop water gently till the surface sink,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And if ye find . . . Ah God, I know not, I! . . .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bedded in store of rotten fig-leaves soft,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And corded up in a tight olive-frail,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some lump, ah God, of lapis lazuli,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Big as a Jewן¿½s head cut off at the nape,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blue as a vein oן¿½er the Madonnaן¿½s breast . . .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sons, all have I bequeathed you, villas, all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That brave Frascati villa with its bath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, let the blue lump poise between my knees,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like God the Fatherן¿½s globe on both His hands
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ye worship in the Jesu Church so gay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Gandolf shall not choose but see and burst!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Swift as a weaverן¿½s shuttle fleet our years:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Man goeth to the grave, and where is he?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did I say basalt for my slab, sons? Black,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas ever antique-black I meant! How else
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall ye contrast my frieze to come beneath?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The bas-relief in bronze ye promised me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Those Pans and Nymphs ye wot of, and perchance
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some tripod, thyrsus, with a vase or so,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Saviour at his sermon on the mount,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saint Praxed in a glory, and one Pan
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ready to twitch the Nymphן¿½s last garment off,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Moses with the tables . . . but I know
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ye mark me not! What do they whisper thee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Child of my bowels, Anselm? Ah, ye hope
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To revel down my villas while I gasp
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bricked oן¿½er with beggarן¿½s mouldy travertine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which Gandolf from his tomb-top chuckles at!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nay, boys, ye love me, all of jasper, then!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis jasper ye stand pledged to, lest I grieve.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My bath must needs be left behind, alas!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One block, pure green as a pistachio-nut,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s plenty jasper somewhere in the world,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And have I not Saint Praxedן¿½s ear to pray
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Horses for ye, and brown Greek manuscripts,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And mistresses with great smooth marbly limbs?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s if ye carve my epitaph aright,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Choice Latin, picked phrase, Tullyן¿½s every word,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No gaudy ware like Gandolfן¿½s second line,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tully, my masters? Ulpian serves his need!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then how I shall lie through centuries,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hear the blessed mutter of the mass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And see God made and eaten all day long,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And feel the steady candle-flame, and taste
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Good strong thick stupefying incense-smoke!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For as I lie here, hours of the dead night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dying in state and by such slow degrees,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I fold my arms as if they clasped a crook,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And stretch my feet forth straight as stone can point,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And let the bedclothes, for a mortcloth, drop
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into great laps and folds of sculptorן¿½s-work:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as yon tapers dwindle, and strange thoughts
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grow, with a certain humming in my ears,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ About the life before I lived this life,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And this life too, popes, cardinals and priests,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saint Praxed at his sermon on the mount,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your tall pale mother with her talking eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And new-found agate urns as fresh as day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And marbleן¿½s language, Latin pure, discreet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Aha, ELUCESCEBAT quoth our friend?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No Tully, said I, Ulpian at the best!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Evil and brief hath been my pilgrimage.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All lapis, all, sons! Else I give the Pope
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My villas! Will ye ever eat my heart?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ever your eyes were as a lizardן¿½s quick,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They glitter like your motherן¿½s for my soul,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or ye would heighten my impoverished frieze,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Piece out its starved design, and fill my vase
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With grapes, and add a vizor and a Term,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And to the tripod ye would tie a lynx
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That in his struggle throws the thyrsus down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To comfort me on my entablature
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereon I am to lie till I must ask
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Do I live, am I dead?ן¿½ There, leave me, there!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For ye have stabbed me with ingratitude
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To death, ye wish it, God, ye wish it! Stone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gritstone, a-crumble! Clammy squares which sweat
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if the corpse they keep were oozing through,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And no more lapis to delight the world!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, go! I bless ye. Fewer tapers there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But in a row: and, going, turn your backs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ay, like departing altar-ministrants,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And leave me in my church, the church for peace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That I may watch at leisure if he leers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Gandolf, at me, from his onion-stone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As still he envied me, so fair she was!
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
